edges.description: Massive uric acid overproduction leads to crystal deposition in joints, kidneys, and urinary tract.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: Purine metabolic disruption leads to dopaminergic dysfunction through incompletely understood mechanisms.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: HPRT1 deficiency disrupts mitochondrial energy metabolism through complex I inhibition, though the precise intermediary steps are not fully elucidated.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: Accelerated de novo purine synthesis leads to accumulation of the intermediate ZMP/AICAR under physiological folate conditions.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: HPRT deficiency causes uric acid overproduction detectable as hyperuricemia.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: HPRT deficiency causes uric acid overproduction detectable as hyperuricosuria.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: Loss-of-function HPRT1 variants cause absent or near-absent HPRT enzyme activity.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: HPRT deficiency is associated with megaloblastic anemia, but the precise intermediate mechanism is not specified in cached evidence.
edges.source: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

edges.description: HPRT-deficiency-driven uric acid overproduction causes hyperuricemia.
Uric acid overproduction leads to monosodium urate crystal arthropathy.
Urate excess causes uric acid nephrolithiasis in the urinary tract.
Urate lithiasis and urinary tract crystal passage can cause hematuria.
intermediate_mechanisms: uric acid lithiasis and urinary tract crystal passage

edges.description: Uric acid crystal nephropathy and nephrolithiasis can progress to renal insufficiency.
Massive uric acid overproduction causes monosodium urate crystal deposition as tophi.
Uric acid crystal nephropathy and tophaceous gout can acutely impair kidney function.
Basal ganglia dopaminergic dysfunction contributes to the severe movement disorder dominated by generalized dystonia.
Basal ganglia dysfunction contributes to the hyperkinetic movement disorder that includes choreoathetosis.
Basal-ganglia motor-circuit dysfunction contributes to the broader abnormal-tone and poorly controlled movement phenotype.
Basal ganglia dysfunction is implicated in the hallmark compulsive self-injury phenotype.
Basal-ganglia and associated cortical circuit dysfunction contributes to the cognitive component of the LND neurobehavioral phenotype.
Basal-ganglia-associated frontostriatal dysfunction contributes to attention and executive-function deficits.
HPRT-related neurodevelopmental abnormalities include early global developmental delay, with the exact intermediate steps still incompletely resolved.
Basal-ganglia motor circuit dysfunction contributes to ballismus within the extrapyramidal movement-disorder spectrum.
HPRT deficiency can include spasticity as part of its neurological disability, but the precise intermediate mechanism is not resolved in cached evidence.
Dystonia and spasticity affecting orofacial motor control can produce dysarthria.
intermediate_mechanisms: orofacial motor impairment from dystonia and spasticity

edges.description: Dystonia and spasticity affecting oropharyngeal motor control can produce dysphagia.
intermediate_mechanisms: oropharyngeal motor impairment from dystonia and spasticity

edges.description: Hemiplegia or hemiparesis is recorded as a very frequent motor phenotype, but the precise causal intermediate is not specified in cached evidence.

edges.target: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

evidence.snippet: enzymatic (HPRT activity determination in haemolysate, intact erythrocytes or fibroblasts) and molecular tests

nodes.description: Acute renal failure can occur secondary to uric acid crystal nephropathy, occasionally as a presenting feature in infancy.

evidence.reference_title: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome presenting with renal insufficiency in infancy and transient neonatal hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: Acute renal failure secondary to crystal nephropathy and tophaceous gout are unusual presenting features of this rare condition.

nodes.description: Xanthine oxidase inhibitor that reduces uric acid production. Controls hyperuricemia, gout, and prevents urate nephropathy. Does not affect neurological or behavioral features.

evidence.snippet: Uric acid overproduction can be managed by allopurinol treatment.

nodes.description: Pharmacologic treatment used to manage spasticity and dystonia in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Spasticity, when present, and dystonia can be managed with benzodiazepines and gamma-aminobutyric acid inhibitors such as baclofen.

nodes.description: HPRT deficiency leads to profound dopaminergic dysfunction in the basal ganglia, with loss of dopaminergic neurotransmitter phenotype and reduced dopamine content in the striatum. This dopamine deficit underlies the movement disorder (dystonia, choreoathetosis) and is hypothesized to contribute to the self-injurious behavior through disrupted reward circuitry. Importantly, this reflects loss of dopaminergic phenotype rather than neuronal degeneration.

evidence.snippet: neurochemical studies have demonstrated 60-90% reductions in the dopamine content of the basal ganglia
neurons of the substantia nigra from the LND cases showed reduced melanization and reduced immunoreactivity for tyrosine hydroxylase (TH), the rate-limiting enzyme in dopamine synthesis
These findings support the proposal that many of the neurobehavioral features of LND might be related to dysfunction of the basal ganglia.
One of the hallmarks of LNS is maximal expression of HPRT in the central nervous system with the highest activity of this enzyme in the midbrain and basal ganglia.

nodes.description: Physical restraints, protective equipment (arm splints, helmets, lip guards), and environmental modifications are essential to prevent self-mutilation. Patients often request their own restraints.

evidence.snippet: Self-injurious behaviour must be managed by a combination of physical restraints, behavioural and pharmaceutical treatments.

nodes.description: Involuntary choreiform and athetoid movements affecting limbs and trunk.

evidence.snippet: Neurological manifestations include severe action dystonia, choreoathetosis, ballismus, cognitive and attention deficit, and self-injurious behaviour.

nodes.description: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) targeting the globus pallidus internus (GPi) has been explored as a treatment for medically resistant dystonia and self-injurious behavior in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome. DBS may reduce the severity of dystonia and decrease self-injurious episodes in selected patients who do not respond to pharmacological and behavioral interventions.

evidence.snippet: DBS improved dystonia of the LNS to varying degrees. All the included cases achieved partial or complete control of self-injurious behavior (SIB). Overall, DBS is a promising treatment for both motor and behavior disorders of LNS patients, but the results reported thus far have varied widely, especially for motor outcomes.

nodes.description: Speech articulation disorder resulting from dystonia and spasticity affecting the orofacial musculature. Most patients have severely impaired or absent speech.

evidence.snippet: Physical rehabilitation, including management of dysarthria and dysphagia, special devices to enable hand control, appropriate walking aids, and a programme of posture management to prevent deformities are recommended.

nodes.description: Swallowing difficulty resulting from dystonia and spasticity affecting the oropharyngeal musculature. May necessitate modified diet consistency or gastrostomy feeding.

evidence.snippet: Physical rehabilitation, including management of dysarthria and dysphagia, special devices to enable hand control, appropriate walking aids, and a programme of posture management to prevent deformities are recommended.

nodes.description: Generalized dystonia is a prominent feature, contributing to significant motor disability. Onset typically in the first year of life.

evidence.snippet: Neurological manifestations include severe action dystonia, choreoathetosis, ballismus, cognitive and attention deficit, and self-injurious behaviour.
a characteristic neurobehavioral phenotype that includes a movement disorder dominated by generalized dystonia, intellectual disability, and recurrent self-injurious behavior.
Uric acid overproduction is present inall HPRT-deficient patients and is associated with lithiasis and gout.
The diagnosis is based on clinical and biochemical findings (hyperuricemia and hyperuricosuria associated with psychomotor delay)

nodes.description: Delayed motor and cognitive milestones, typically noted by 3-6 months of age with hypotonia and delayed sitting.

evidence.snippet: The characteristics defining the disease are hyperuricemia, neurodevelopmental abnormalities with global developmental delay, involuntary movements, and self-injurious behavior.

nodes.description: Urate crystal arthropathy may develop, particularly if hyperuricemia is untreated. Allopurinol controls this manifestation effectively.

evidence.snippet: LND is characterized by overproduction of uric acid, leading to gouty arthritis and nephrolithiasis.
HP:0001997 | Gout | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Loss of HPRT activity abolishes the purine salvage pathway, preventing recycling of hypoxanthine and guanine to IMP and GMP. This leads to accumulation of hypoxanthine, which is oxidized to uric acid by xanthine oxidase. Additionally, phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate (PRPP) accumulates due to lack of consumption by HPRT, driving de novo purine synthesis and further increasing uric acid production.
nodes.id: HPRT1 Enzyme Deficiency and Purine Overproduction

evidence.snippet: The enzyme is responsible for recycling purines by converting guanine and hypoxanthine into guanosine monophosphate and inosine monophosphate, respectively. Lack of the enzyme causes an increase in guanine and hypoxanthine, which eventually is converted into uric acid.
The pathogenesis of overproduction of uric acid in HPRT deficiency is well-understood, and treatments are available to control it.
To compensate this deficiency, there is an acceleration of the de novo purine biosynthetic pathway.

nodes.description: Partial HPRT deficiency (1.5-8% residual activity) from missense mutations that reduce but do not abolish enzyme function. Presents with hyperuricemia and gout but without the neurological or behavioral features of classic LNS.
Complete loss of HPRT enzyme activity (<1.5% of normal) from null, frameshift, nonsense, or splice-site mutations. Over 300 disease-associated mutations identified across the gene.

evidence.snippet: Human HPRT is encoded by a single structural gene on the long arm of the X chromosome at Xq26. To date, more than 300 disease-associated mutations in the HPRT1 gene have been identified.
Lesch Nyhan syndrome is an inborn disorder caused by a deficiency of hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HPRT) enzyme, an enzyme of the purine salvage pathway.
HPRT1 | hypoxanthine phosphoribosyltransferase 1 | hgnc:5157 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) (loss of function) in
HPRT1 | HGNC:5157 | Lesch-Nyhan syndrome | MONDO:0010298 | XL | Definitive

nodes.description: Hematuria may occur secondary to uric acid crystal formation and passage through the urinary tract, or from urate nephrolithiasis.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000790 | Hematuria | Frequent (79-30%)
Uric acid overproduction is present inall HPRT-deficient patients and is associated with lithiasis and gout.

nodes.description: Involuntary flinging movements of the extremities, typically affecting proximal limb muscles. Part of the hyperkinetic movement disorder spectrum in LNS alongside dystonia and choreoathetosis.

evidence.snippet: Neurological manifestations include severe action dystonia, choreoathetosis, ballismus, cognitive and attention deficit, and self-injurious behaviour.

nodes.description: Unilateral motor weakness reflecting pyramidal tract involvement from upper motor neuron dysfunction. May be asymmetric and contribute to functional impairment alongside the extrapyramidal movement disorder.

evidence.snippet: HP:0004374 | Hemiplegia/hemiparesis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Increased muscle tone emerges during the first year of life alongside the poorly controlled movements, contributing to the early motor disability of classic Lesch-Nyhan disease.

evidence.reference_title: Update on the phenotypic spectrum of Lesch-Nyhan disease and its attenuated variants.
evidence.snippet: Poorly controlled movements along with increased muscle tone also emerge during the first year of life.

nodes.description: Markedly elevated serum uric acid levels, often exceeding 10 mg/dL, resulting from both increased de novo purine synthesis and failure of purine salvage.

evidence.snippet: Uric acid overproduction is present inall HPRT-deficient patients and is associated with lithiasis and gout.
HP:0002149 | Hyperuricemia | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Massively elevated serum uric acid leads to monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints (gout), urinary tract (nephrolithiasis), and kidneys (urate nephropathy), potentially causing renal failure if untreated.

evidence.snippet: All of these phenotypes are associated with marked overproduction of uric acid and related problems such as hyperuricemia, urate nephrolithiasis, tophi, and gout.
Uric acid overproduction is present inall HPRT-deficient patients and is associated with lithiasis and gout.

nodes.description: Moderate to severe intellectual disability is present in classic LNS. Cognitive function may be underestimated due to severe motor and behavioral limitations that interfere with testing.

evidence.snippet: The most severe phenotype is known as Lesch-Nyhan disease, in which the phenotype also includes severe motor handicap, intellectual disability, and self-injurious behavior.
HP:0002342 | Intellectual disability, moderate | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Megaloblastic erythropoiesis can occur due to impaired purine nucleotide availability for DNA synthesis.

evidence.snippet: Megaloblastic anaemia is also associated with the disease.
HP:0001903 | Anemia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: HPRT1 deficiency inhibits complex I-dependent mitochondrial respiration, resulting in increased mitochondrial NADH levels, reduction of mitochondrial membrane potential, and increased rate of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. However, this increased ROS production does not induce oxidative stress or deplete endogenous glutathione, suggesting that disruption of mitochondrial energy metabolism rather than oxidative stress is the potential trigger of brain pathology in LNS.

evidence.snippet: We found that HPRT1 deficiency inhibits complex I-dependent mitochondrial respiration resulting in increased levels of mitochondrial NADH, reduction of the mitochondrial membrane potential, and increased rate of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in mitochondria and cytosol.
Thus, disruption of mitochondrial energy metabolism but not oxidative stress could play a role of potential trigger of brain pathology in LNS.

nodes.description: Uric acid nephrolithiasis may be the presenting feature, sometimes detected as orange crystalluria in infancy.

evidence.snippet: All of these phenotypes are associated with marked overproduction of uric acid and related problems such as hyperuricemia, urate nephrolithiasis, tophi, and gout.

nodes.description: Management of dysarthria, dysphagia, walking aids, hand control devices, and posture management to prevent deformities.

evidence.snippet: Physical rehabilitation, including management of dysarthria and dysphagia, special devices to enable hand control, appropriate walking aids, and a programme of posture management to prevent deformities are recommended.

nodes.description: Progressive renal insufficiency from urate nephropathy may develop if hyperuricemia is untreated.

evidence.snippet: Acute renal failure secondary to crystal nephropathy and tophaceous gout are unusual presenting features of this rare condition.
HP:0000083 | Renal insufficiency | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Compulsive self-mutilation is the hallmark behavioral feature, typically emerging between ages 2-4 years. Includes lip and finger biting severe enough to cause tissue loss. Patients are often distressed by their own behavior and may request physical restraints.

evidence.snippet: The most severe phenotype is known as Lesch-Nyhan disease, in which the phenotype also includes severe motor handicap, intellectual disability, and self-injurious behavior.
Symptoms of LNS include dystonia, gout, intellectual disability, and self-mutilation.

nodes.description: Attention deficit is recognised among the cognitive manifestations of HPRT deficiency, distinct from the global intellectual disability.

evidence.snippet: Neurological manifestations include severe action dystonia, choreoathetosis, ballismus, cognitive and attention deficit, and self-injurious behaviour.

nodes.description: Upper motor neuron spasticity contributing to pyramidal signs and motor impairment.

evidence.snippet: Spasticity, when present, and dystonia can be managed with benzodiazepines and gamma-aminobutyric acid inhibitors such as baclofen.
HP:0001257 | Spasticity | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Deposition of monosodium urate crystals forming tophi, a consequence of the massive uric acid overproduction that characterizes HPRT deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Update on the phenotypic spectrum of Lesch-Nyhan disease and its attenuated variants.
evidence.snippet: All of these phenotypes are associated with marked overproduction of uric acid and related problems such as hyperuricemia, urate nephrolithiasis, tophi, and gout.

evidence.reference_title: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome presenting with renal insufficiency in infancy and transient neonatal hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: Acute renal failure secondary to crystal nephropathy and tophaceous gout are unusual presenting features of this rare condition.

nodes.description: Under physiological folate conditions, LND fibroblasts accumulate 5-aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide riboside 5'-monophosphate (ZMP/AICAR), an intermediate of the de novo purine biosynthetic pathway. ZMP accumulation activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) and inhibits adenylosuccinate lyase (ADSL). LND fibroblasts also show metabolic adaptations including higher glycolytic capacity, increased expression of the folate carrier SLC19A1, decreased mitochondrial potential, and reduced cell migration. These alterations can be reversed with high levels of folic acid. Note: these findings are based on in vitro fibroblast data and CNS relevance has not been established in vivo.

evidence.snippet: LND fibroblasts maintained with Plasmax-PV show metabolic adaptations such a higher glycolytic capacity, increased expression of the folate carrier SCL19A1, and functional alterations such a decreased mitochondrial potential and reduced cell migration compared to controls.
These alterations can be reverted with high levels of folic acid, suggesting that folic acid supplements might be a potential treatment for LND.
